front: What is the primary function of an encoder in digital logic?
back: An encoder converts information from one format to another. Specifically, it takes 2^n input lines and generates an n-bit binary code at its output, representing the active input line.

front: What is a key limitation of a simple 4-to-2 binary encoder?
back: A simple 4-to-2 encoder assumes that only one input is active (HIGH) at any given time. If more than one input is active, the output can be incorrect or invalid.

front: What is the purpose of a priority encoder?
back: A priority encoder resolves the issue of multiple active inputs by assigning a priority level to each input (usually with the highest number having the highest priority). It outputs the binary code corresponding to the highest-priority active input.

front: What does the 'Valid Output' (V) or 'Enable Output' signal indicate in a priority encoder?
back: The Valid Output (V) signal indicates whether any input is active. It is HIGH when at least one input is active, ensuring the output code is meaningful. It is LOW when all inputs are LOW (inactive).

front: How does an encoder fundamentally differ from a decoder?
back: They are inverse operations. An encoder has multiple inputs and fewer outputs; it generates a binary code based on which single input is activated. A decoder has few inputs and many outputs; it activates a single output line based on the binary code presented at its input.
